key_points: Sound is a mechanical longitudinal wave produced by vibrating bodies and requires a medium for propagation.
Speed of sound varies in solids, liquids, and gases, and increases with temperature; v ∝ √T for gases.
The relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength: v = f × λ. Changes in medium or temperature affect speed and wavelength but not frequency.
Resonance occurs when a system vibrates at its natural frequency due to an external force of the same frequency, amplifying sound.
Doppler effect: change in frequency and pitch of sound due to relative motion between source and observer; f' = f (v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs).
Ultrasound (>20 kHz) and infrasound (<20 Hz) have specific applications: medical imaging, SONAR, industrial cleaning, earthquake detection.
Echo, reverberation, reflection, refraction, diffraction, and absorption of sound are critical in acoustics; e.g., auditoriums are designed to minimize reverberation time.
Musical instruments: frequency depends on length, tension, and mass per unit length of string or column; harmonics produce overtones.
Intensity and loudness: intensity ∝ amplitude²; logarithmic scale (decibel, dB) is used to measure sound level; threshold of hearing ≈ 10⁻¹² W/m².
formulas: Speed of sound: v = d / t
Doppler effect: f' = f (v ± vo)/(v ∓ vs)
Fundamental frequency of open air column: f = v / 2L; closed: f = v / 4L
Reverberation time (Sabine’s formula): T = 0.161 V / A, where V = volume of hall, A = total absorption
examples: If a source of 500 Hz moves towards an observer at 10 m/s, speed of sound 343 m/s: f' = 500 × (343 + 0)/(343 − 10) ≈ 514.9 Hz.
An ultrasound of 2 MHz is used to image a fetus; wavelength λ = v / f = 1540 / 2×10⁶ ≈ 0.77 mm.
Echo from a cliff 680 m away is heard after 4 s: v = 2×680/4 = 340 m/s.
A 0.85 m open pipe produces fundamental note: f = 343 / 1.7 ≈ 201.8 Hz.
Reverberation time of a hall: V = 500 m³, A = 250 m² absorption → T = 0.161 × 500 / 250 ≈ 0.322 s.
exam_tips: Practice Doppler effect problems carefully; note signs for source and observer motion.
Always check units when using wave formulas (v in m/s, f in Hz, λ in m).
Remember resonance conditions for strings and air columns for harmonics.
Reverberation, echo, and reflection questions may appear as real-life applications.
For intensity and loudness, use logarithmic relation if asked for decibels; threshold values are important.
